# 6.5 Visualizing Continuous Variables Across Groups {.unnumbered}

Sometimes we want to compare the distribution of a continuous variable across categories of another variable. For example, we might want to visualize boredom scores by class level or mind wandering scores by gender.

This kind of graph usually combines:

- a continuous outcome variable
- a categorical grouping variable

## Useful Graph Options

Personally, I love checking all four options: box plot, violin, data, and mean. Each one shows something a little different.

- The box plot shows the median, quartiles, spread, and possible outliers.
- The violin plot shows the shape of the distribution.
- The data points show individual observations, which can reveal patterns the summaries hide.
- The mean marker shows the average for each group.

You will not always need all four in a final report, but when you are exploring your data, seeing more information is often helpful.

## Why Show Individual Data Points?

I like showing jittered data points because they make the sample visible. A mean and standard deviation can hide small sample sizes, unusual values, or uneven group sizes.

For example, if one group has only one person, a mean exists, but it does not tell us much about a group pattern. Seeing the data points helps us notice that.

## What to Look For

When visualizing continuous variables across groups, ask:

- Do groups have similar or different centers?
- Do groups differ in spread?
- Are some groups much smaller than others?
- Are there possible outliers?
- Are the distributions skewed within groups?
- Does the graph suggest a pattern worth testing later?

Remember, visual group differences are descriptive. They help us understand the sample. They do not, by themselves, tell us whether a difference is statistically significant.

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Do not conclude that groups are statistically different based only on a graph. Graphs help us see patterns. Inferential tests help us evaluate statistical evidence.
:::
